name: Yanluo Wang
description: Yanluo Wang, also known as Yama, is a prominent deity in Chinese mythology and the ruler of Diyu, the underworld realm where souls are judged after death. He is a figure of immense power and authority, overseeing the ten courts of hell, each dedicated to specific tortures and punishments tailored to the sins committed during a mortal's life. Originally, Yanluo Wang was believed to be a mortal who, due to his righteousness and impartiality, was appointed as the judge of the dead. Over time, this mortal figure evolved into a powerful deity, often depicted as a stern and imposing figure with a judge's robe and a stern expression. His role is not solely punitive; he also presides over the cycle of reincarnation, determining the fate of souls and their subsequent rebirths based on their karmic merit. He is assisted by a vast bureaucracy of officials and demons who carry out his judgments, ensuring cosmic justice is served.
subtitle: Judge of the Dead

keyMyths.title: Yanluo Wang's Mortal Origins
keyMyths.description: One myth recounts that Yanluo Wang was once a just and compassionate mortal ruler who, during a time of great famine, sacrificed himself to provide sustenance for his people. His selfless act earned him the respect of the celestial court, and he was appointed as the judge of the dead, tasked with ensuring that all souls received fair judgment based on their deeds in life. This tale emphasizes his initial virtue and the divine recognition of his inherent justice.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E. T. C. *Myths and Legends of China*. George G. Harrap & Company, 1922.

keyMyths.title: The Ten Courts of Hell
keyMyths.description: The Ten Courts of Hell are each presided over by a different judge under Yanluo Wang's authority. Each court is dedicated to a specific type of sin and employs unique and often gruesome methods of torture designed to cleanse the soul before reincarnation. Souls pass through each court, facing judgment and punishment appropriate to their transgressions, before ultimately being assigned their next life based on their remaining karma. This system ensures a meticulous accounting of all deeds, both good and bad.
keyMyths.source: Teiser, Stephen. *The Scripture on the Ten Kings*. University of Hawaii Press, 1994.

keyMyths.title: The Scroll of Life and Death
keyMyths.description: Yanluo Wang possesses the Scroll of Life and Death, a divine record that contains the names of every mortal, their lifespan, and the circumstances of their death. This scroll is consulted during judgment to determine the fate of each soul. It's believed that by manipulating or altering this scroll, one could potentially cheat death or extend their lifespan, though such actions are often depicted as leading to disastrous consequences. The scroll represents the predetermined nature of fate and the limits of mortal agency.
keyMyths.source: De Groot, J. J. M. *The Religious System of China*. Brill, 1892.

sources.author: Werner, E. T. C.

sources.description: An in-depth analysis of the Ten Kings of Hell, shedding light on the structure of the underworld and the judgment process overseen by Yanluo Wang.
